evidence: The codebase has a clear two-zone architecture: scripts/ (standalone CLI tools) and templates/ (SDK-based app scaffolds). Scripts have zero fan-in/fan-out between each other, which is clean.
Each template is fully self-contained with its own bags-client.ts and index.ts, no cross-template imports exist. This is good boundary isolation.

The loadConfig() function is duplicated identically across all 7 scripts (analyze.ts, fees.ts, pools.ts, quote.ts, setup.ts, social-lookup.ts, token-info.ts) rather than shared from a common module. This is cross-package duplication indicating a missing shared boundary.
API_BASE and CONFIG_PATH constants are duplicated across all 7 scripts with identical values.

The socialLookup function in fee-sharing-app/src/bags-client.ts uses raw fetch to hit the same API endpoint that scripts/social-lookup.ts calls, duplicating the URL and response parsing logic across boundaries.
templates/trading-bot/src/bags-client.ts getRecentLaunches() also uses raw fetch with a hardcoded URL, duplicating the API_BASE pattern from scripts/
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: Cross-script duplication of loadConfig(), API_BASE, and CONFIG_PATH across all 7 scripts indicates a missing shared utility module. Raw fetch fallbacks in template bags-client files duplicate API access patterns.

evidence: Top-level structure maps cleanly to domain: scripts/ = CLI exploration tools, templates/ = starter app scaffolds. A new engineer would immediately understand the purpose of each.
Each template directory maps to a distinct Bags use case: fee-sharing-app, token-launcher, trading-bot. Names communicate purpose clearly.
Script files map 1:1 to API capabilities: analyze.ts, fees.ts, pools.ts, quote.ts, setup.ts, social-lookup.ts, token-info.ts. Each does one thing.
Template internal structure is consistent: bags-client.ts (API layer) + index.ts (entry point/re-exports). This pattern is predictable across all 3 templates.
Public surface is minimal: each template exports only the functions/types needed from bags-client.ts via index.ts barrel.
plugin.json describes the project accurately as a 'Full-stack developer toolkit for building on Bags.fm'.
high_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: The fee-sharing-app template's bags-client.ts includes socialLookup() which is not specific to fee sharing, slightly blurring ownership. Template index.ts files are predominantly comments (91% and 88% comment ratios) acting more as documentation files than entry points.

evidence: Naming convention is consistently camelCase across both scripts/ and templates/src/. No snake_case drift detected.

All scripts follow the same structural pattern: shebang, JSDoc header, imports (fs, path, os), constants (API_BASE, CONFIG_PATH), loadConfig(), parseArgs() (where needed), main(), and error handler. This is highly consistent.
All template bags-client.ts files use named exports consistently. index.ts files use named re-exports. No default exports anywhere.
Error message formatting differs: scripts use console.error('Failed:', ...) or console.error('API error (...)'), inconsistent phrasing across sibling scripts.

scripts/analyze.ts uses a safeFetch() wrapper pattern with null returns, while all other scripts use inline fetch with if (!data.success) checks. This is a behavioral protocol divergence among siblings.

scripts/fees.ts handles both array and object response shapes with a complex entries normalization (lines 62-66), while other scripts assume the response shape. Inconsistent defensive coding across siblings.

Template index.ts files have dramatically different content: fee-sharing-app and token-launcher index.ts are almost entirely comments with a single export line, while trading-bot index.ts has actual application logic (polling loop, state management).

Scripts use two distinct error strategies: analyze.ts returns null from safeFetch (tolerating partial failures), while all other scripts use if (!data.success) { console.error; process.exit(1) } (fail-fast). These are valid but different philosophies applied to siblings.
Template bags-client.ts files consistently throw errors, which is appropriate for library code. This is a deliberate layer-based distinction from scripts.
fees.ts (line 43) calls res.json() without checking res.ok first, meaning a 500 response with non-JSON body will throw an unhandled parse error rather than a meaningful error message.
pools.ts (lines 49, 63), quote.ts (line 66), social-lookup.ts (line 58), token-info.ts (lines 52-53, 64, 73) all have the same pattern: fetch without res.ok check before res.json().

trading-bot/src/index.ts checkForNewLaunches() catches errors and logs them (line 37: console.error('Poll error:', error)) but silently continues. The error is swallowed with no recovery or back-off strategy.

trading-bot/src/bags-client.ts getRecentLaunches() returns empty array on failure (line 90: data.success ? data.response : []), silently converting errors to empty data. Callers cannot distinguish 'no launches' from 'API failure'.
fee-sharing-app/src/bags-client.ts socialLookup() throws on !data.success (line 84) but does not check res.ok, so a network error or non-JSON response throws an unhelpful parse error.
token-launcher/src/bags-client.ts checkDexscreenerAvailability() has the same res.ok gap (line 97-98).
Mixed error context preservation: template throws include the API error message (data.error || 'fallback'), but script console.errors use varying message formats with no structured error type.
File names are descriptive and domain-aligned: analyze.ts, fees.ts, pools.ts, quote.ts, setup.ts, social-lookup.ts, token-info.ts. Each name communicates the script's purpose.

Function names in template bags-client.ts files are specific and behavior-revealing: createFeeShareConfig, prepareLaunch, checkDexscreenerAvailability, getClaimablePositions, getLifetimeFees, getRecentLaunches.
resolveMint() in quote.ts clearly communicates its purpose of resolving SOL/WSOL aliases to mint addresses.
safeFetch() in analyze.ts communicates that the fetch is error-tolerant (returns null instead of throwing).
The 'main' function name is used across all scripts, which is conventional for CLI entry points.
LaunchConfig interface in token-launcher uses clear, specific field names (creatorWallet, initialBuyLamports, claimers, splits).
WSOL constant is well-named, using the standard Solana abbreviation for Wrapped SOL.
parseArgs() in pools.ts and token-info.ts is descriptive but generic; however, since each file has exactly one arg-parsing function, the name is unambiguous in context.
Variable 'entries' in fees.ts (line 62) is somewhat generic for what represents normalized claimable positions.
Variable 'flags' in pools.ts and token-info.ts uses Record<string, string | boolean> which loses the structure information; the variable name communicates intent adequately though.
naming_quality.issues_preventing_higher_score: A few local variable names (entries, flags, pos) lean generic in context-specific code. The 'bags' export name in template clients is terse and could be more descriptive for external consumers.

evidence: Template bags-client.ts files wrap SDK methods (e.g. getQuote, getClaimablePositions) with thin functions that add validation (split length/total checks in createFeeShareConfig) or compose multi-step flows (prepareLaunch orchestrates 3 SDK calls). These are not pure pass-throughs.
No interfaces or types with single implementations and no polymorphic pressure. LaunchConfig is the only custom interface and it is consumed directly by prepareLaunch.
Scripts use no abstraction layers at all; they directly call fetch and format output. No unnecessary indirection.

The fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts has 3 functions (getQuote, getClaimablePositions, getLifetimeFees) that are thin SDK wrappers with no added validation, translation, or error handling beyond what the SDK provides. These add naming convenience but no behavioral value.

Indirection hotspots flagged mechanically (chain_depth 2 in bags-client files) are SDK method calls (bags.trade.getQuote, bags.config.createBagsFeeShareConfig), which is normal SDK usage, not problematic indirection.
abstraction_fitness.issues_preventing_higher_score: Three thin SDK wrapper functions in fee-sharing-app/src/bags-client.ts add naming ceremony without validation, translation, or error enrichment. They re-export SDK methods under slightly different names without earning the indirection.

evidence: Each template has a focused dependency set: @bagsfm/bags-sdk as core, @solana/web3.js for blockchain operations, and framework-specific deps (next, react for web apps; bs58 for the trading bot).
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose across any template. All templates use the native fetch API rather than pulling in axios or similar.
The trading-bot template correctly adds bs58 for base58 encoding/decoding of Solana transactions, which is a necessary dependency for its use case.
No circular dependency cycles visible in the import graph. Each template is self-contained with no cross-template imports.
All three templates pin @bagsfm/bags-sdk to ^1.3.1, ensuring version consistency across templates.
Scripts directory has no package.json at all, relying on global tsx availability, which is appropriate for CLI scripts invoked via npx.
dependency_health.issues_preventing_higher_score: The fee-sharing-app and token-launcher templates include @privy-io/react-auth as a dependency but neither template contains any code that imports or uses Privy. This is a speculative dependency that adds install weight without current value.

evidence: The safeFetch helper in scripts/analyze.ts is well-crafted: clear control flow (check res.ok, check data.success, catch), returns null on all error paths, and truncates URLs in error messages. This is the best implementation pattern in the scripts.

scripts/fees.ts main() has a complex response-shape normalization block (lines 62-66) that handles both array and object response shapes with a ternary chain. This defensive handling is necessary but the code is dense and could benefit from a named extraction.

scripts/quote.ts hardcodes decimal assumptions (line 53: decimals = inputMint === WSOL ? 9 : 6) which is acknowledged in a comment but creates a silent correctness bug for tokens with non-6 decimal places.
scripts/token-info.ts has inconsistent error handling across its three branches: the --mint branch does no error checking on the fetch responses, while --top and default branches check data.success.
trading-bot/src/bags-client.ts swap() function is well-structured: linear flow of get quote, optionally execute, sign, simulate, submit. Each step builds on the previous with clear early return.
token-launcher/src/bags-client.ts prepareLaunch() follows a clean sequential flow with validation at the top and clear step numbering in comments.
Helper extraction is consistent across scripts: all use the same loadConfig() pattern and the same main().catch() wrapper. This consistency aids readability.
Templates have a clean two-file structure: bags-client.ts handles API interaction and index.ts re-exports the public surface. This is a simple, predictable seam.
The scripts directory has no cross-module boundaries; each script is self-contained with its own loadConfig, fetch calls, and output formatting. No seam quality issues because there are no seams.

Template bags-client files handle API boundary translation cleanly: prepareLaunch in token-launcher transforms a flat LaunchConfig into sequential SDK calls and assembles the result. Data translation happens in one obvious place.
The trading-bot index.ts orchestrates via simple composition: getRecentLaunches() feeds into swap() with a seen-set filter between them. This reads as clean collaboration.

The fee-sharing-app socialLookup function bypasses the SDK and makes a raw fetch call to the API, while sibling functions use the SDK. This creates an inconsistent boundary pattern within the same module, where some functions go through the SDK abstraction and one goes around it.

Scripts duplicate the loadConfig/API_BASE/CONFIG_PATH pattern across all 7 files rather than sharing a common module. While each file is self-contained, this means boundary contracts (config shape, API base URL) are implicitly duplicated rather than explicitly shared.
Zero test files exist anywhere in the repository. No .test.ts, .spec.ts, __tests__ directories, or test configuration files (jest.config, vitest.config, etc.) were found.
The codebase has 13 production files totaling 983 LOC with zero test coverage.
All 11 mechanical test_coverage findings confirm every module is untested.

Critical business logic is completely unvalidated: fee calculations with BigInt arithmetic in analyze.ts and fees.ts, basis point validation in fee-sharing-app and token-launcher bags-client.ts, decimal conversion in quote.ts.
The swap() function in trading-bot/bags-client.ts handles real financial transactions (Solana swaps via Jito bundles) with zero test coverage.
prepareLaunch() orchestrates a multi-step token launch flow with validation logic that is never tested.
No test framework is even configured as a devDependency in any package.json.
Scripts use raw fetch with manual response parsing while template bags-client.ts files use the @bagsfm/bags-sdk, creating two different API interaction patterns

fee-sharing-app/bags-client.ts exports getQuote(inputMint, outputMint, amount) taking amount as string, while scripts/quote.ts computes amount internally from float input with hardcoded decimal assumptions
fee-sharing-app/bags-client.ts socialLookup falls back to raw fetch because the endpoint is not in the SDK, mixing SDK and raw fetch in the same module

scripts/ modules have inconsistent error handling at the API surface: analyze.ts uses safeFetch returning null, while fees.ts, pools.ts, quote.ts check data.success inline with different error message formats

token-launcher/bags-client.ts prepareLaunch takes a config object (LaunchConfig), while fee-sharing-app/bags-client.ts createFeeShareConfig takes 4 positional parameters for a similar domain operation
trading-bot/bags-client.ts swap() mixes quote-fetching and execution in one function based on an execute boolean flag, overloading the interface
All scripts consistently use loadConfig() to read the API key from ~/.bags-dev-kit/config.json and pass it as x-api-key header
All template bags-client.ts files consistently check process.env.BAGS_API_KEY at module scope and throw if missing
setup.ts correctly sets config directory permissions to 0o700 and file permissions to 0o600, protecting the API key at rest
trading-bot bags-client.ts validates both BAGS_API_KEY and SOLANA_PRIVATE_KEY environment variables at module scope
No routes/endpoints exist in this codebase; it is a dev kit with scripts and templates, so route-level auth patterns do not apply
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The API key is passed via non-null assertion (process.env.BAGS_API_KEY!) in raw-fetch fallback paths within template bags-client files, bypassing the module-scope validation. Additionally, scripts expose the raw API key in fetch headers without any abstraction that could be swapped for a more secure credential source.

evidence: templates/fee-sharing-app/src/index.ts has a 0.91 comment ratio: 21 of 23 lines are comments/docs, with only a single re-export line and a type re-export as actual code
templates/token-launcher/src/index.ts has a 0.88 comment ratio: similar pattern of extensive doc comments around minimal re-exports
loadConfig() is duplicated verbatim across 6 scripts (analyze.ts, fees.ts, pools.ts, quote.ts, social-lookup.ts, token-info.ts) with identical try-catch and error message

fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts has pass-through wrappers: getQuote just forwards 3 args to bags.trade.getQuote, getClaimablePositions forwards 1 arg to bags.fee.getAllClaimablePositions, getLifetimeFees forwards 1 arg to bags.state.getTokenLifetimeFees
Docstring bloat on trivial functions: getClaimablePositions is a 1-line function with a 3-line JSDoc that just says 'Get all claimable fee positions for a wallet'
Every script has the identical main().catch() error handler pattern copied verbatim: err instanceof Error ? err.message : err
parseArgs() in pools.ts and token-info.ts follow the same structure but are duplicated rather than shared
All source files are TypeScript (.ts) with no mixed JS/TS modules
No deprecated symbols, @deprecated annotations, or DEPRECATED markers found in any file
No TODO/FIXME comments referencing migration, legacy, or 'remove after' patterns, except one benign TODO in trading-bot/src/index.ts line 33 for strategy customization
Consistent use of native fetch across all scripts (no axios/fetch mix)
All templates use @bagsfm/bags-sdk ^1.3.1 consistently
The Bags API reference mentions v2 and v3 endpoint versions, and the code consistently uses the latest versions (creator/v3, wallet/v2, claim-txs/v3)
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: The fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts socialLookup uses raw fetch to hit an endpoint not yet in the SDK, which is a minor form of old/new pattern coexistence. Additionally, trading-bot bags-client.ts getRecentLaunches also uses raw fetch rather than SDK, suggesting the SDK does not fully cover the API surface and these raw-fetch calls may be migration remnants awaiting SDK support.

evidence: Clear top-level split: scripts/ (7 standalone CLI tools), templates/ (3 app templates), references/ (4 docs), commands/ (5 skill commands). Each directory has a clear, distinct purpose.
templates/ uses a clean subdirectory-per-template pattern: fee-sharing-app/, token-launcher/, trading-bot/, each with its own package.json and src/
scripts/ has 7 files at 608 LOC total, well under the 10-file mixed-concern threshold for flat directories
No root-level straggler source files; all TS files live under scripts/ or templates/*/src/
Directory profiles show no cross-directory coupling: scripts have 0 fan-in/fan-out, templates have minimal internal coupling (0.5 avg)
The codebase is 13 files total, well within the <20 file threshold where flat structure is appropriate per the skip rules
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: The scripts/ directory has no shared module despite all 6 async scripts duplicating loadConfig, API_BASE, and CONFIG_PATH. A scripts/shared.ts would reduce duplication and improve navigability. However, this is more of an ai_generated_debt concern than a structural placement issue.

evidence: All three template bags-client.ts files read process.env.BAGS_API_KEY at module scope and throw if missing, making them impossible to import without the env var set
templates/trading-bot/src/bags-client.ts additionally reads SOLANA_PRIVATE_KEY at module scope and creates a Keypair singleton, coupling import to env state
templates/trading-bot/src/bags-client.ts creates a Connection singleton at module scope with process.env.SOLANA_RPC_URL fallback
All scripts/ files compute CONFIG_PATH and API_BASE as module-level constants, though these are pure derivations and not side-effecting
scripts/ loadConfig() reads filesystem at call time (not import time), which is the correct pattern
scripts/ files repeat identical boilerplate patterns: loadConfig(), CONFIG_PATH/API_BASE constants, main().catch() wrapper, and the same error formatting across all 7 files
fees.ts main() handles both array and object response shapes with inline type-narrowing logic that mixes API response parsing with business logic (lamport aggregation)
token-info.ts main() has three distinct code paths (--mint, --top, --feed) each with different fetch/parse/format logic in one function body
fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts has async functions (getQuote, getClaimablePositions, getLifetimeFees) that simply forward to SDK methods without adding behavior
quote.ts hardcodes decimals assumption (SOL=9, else=6) inline rather than treating it as a named concern
scripts/analyze.ts safeFetch returns typed as implicit any (no return type annotation) but actually returns response data | null, which is a contract gap for callers

fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts getQuote, getClaimablePositions, getLifetimeFees are documented as specific operations but their return types are fully delegated to the SDK with no narrowing, so the contract is only as good as the SDK's types

token-launcher bags-client.ts prepareLaunch documents a 3-step flow but returns an object with transactions that may or may not contain feeConfig.transactions (line 82: ...(feeConfig.transactions || [])), making the return shape uncertain

scripts/quote.ts hardcodes decimals=6 for non-SOL tokens (line 53) but the comment on line 51-52 acknowledges this is wrong for tokens with different decimals, creating a documented contract violation
fee-sharing-app socialLookup uses process.env.BAGS_API_KEY! with non-null assertion (line 81) despite the module-level guard already ensuring it exists
fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts: getQuote, getClaimablePositions, getLifetimeFees are async functions that never await, wrapping promises in an unnecessary extra promise layer
scripts/fees.ts lines 62-66: nested ternary for handling array vs object vs null response shapes is functional but harder to follow than explicit if/else blocks
scripts/analyze.ts lines 81-98: fee analysis calculation is clear with well-named intermediate variables (totalFees, totalClaimed, unclaimed, claimRate)
trading-bot/src/index.ts: SEEN_MINTS Set is used correctly to deduplicate across poll cycles, logic is straightforward
scripts/token-info.ts: the three-branch if/else if/else in main() is clear in intent but would benefit from early returns
scripts/fees.ts line 63: positions.map((pos: any, i: number) => ...) uses explicit any for API response elements
scripts/token-info.ts line 84: tokens.map((t: any) => ...) uses explicit any for token feed elements
scripts/analyze.ts line 64: analysis typed as Record<string, unknown> loses all structure
No return type annotations on any script main() functions or helper functions across the codebase
scripts/analyze.ts safeFetch has no return type annotation, returns unknown | null implicitly
token-launcher bags-client.ts prepareLaunch has no return type annotation despite returning a complex object
trading-bot bags-client.ts swap function has no return type annotation despite three different return shapes
LaunchConfig interface in token-launcher is well-typed with clear required/optional fields
fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts createFeeShareConfig has properly typed parameters (string, string, string[], number[])

cross_module_architecture.dimension_character: The codebase has clean boundary isolation between its two major zones (scripts and templates) and between sibling templates. No circular dependencies or hub modules exist. However, the scripts/ directory has significant cross-file duplication of shared infrastructure (loadConfig, API_BASE, CONFIG_PATH) that signals a missing shared boundary, and template bags-client files duplicate raw API access patterns that could be consolidated.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The architecture is fundamentally sound with clear separation and no dependency direction violations. The main drag is the repeated boilerplate across scripts, which is a real cross-module architecture concern (missing shared boundary) but is contained to one zone. Per global anchors, this sits slightly above the 80 'solid but uneven' mark because the boundaries themselves are clean and the duplication, while real, does not create blast radius or coupling risk.

high_level_elegance.dimension_character: The codebase has a clear, domain-aligned decomposition that a developer can grasp immediately. Scripts map to API exploration capabilities, templates map to distinct product use cases, and the internal template structure (bags-client.ts + index.ts) is consistent across all three templates. The ownership boundaries are tight and predictable, with only minor blurring where socialLookup crosses concern boundaries.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: This scores above the 80 'solid but uneven' anchor because the decomposition is intentional, consistent, and domain-aligned throughout. A developer would have no trouble explaining why each file and directory exists. The minor issues (socialLookup placement, comment-heavy index files) are cosmetic rather than structural. Anchoring near 85-86 reflects strong ownership clarity with very minor boundary blur.

convention_outlier.dimension_character: The codebase is strong on surface-level conventions: consistent camelCase naming, consistent export patterns, and consistent file naming. However, behavioral protocols diverge across sibling scripts. analyze.ts uses a unique safeFetch wrapper while siblings use inline checks. fees.ts has uniquely defensive response shape handling. Template index.ts files serve different structural roles (pure re-export vs. application logic). These behavioral inconsistencies mean a developer learning the pattern from one script cannot fully trust it holds in siblings.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: Surface conventions (naming, exports, file structure) are solid, which would push toward 85+. However, the behavioral protocol divergences across sibling scripts (safeFetch pattern in analyze.ts, response shape handling in fees.ts) and the inconsistent role of index.ts across templates pull the score down. Per the 80 anchor ('why is this different?'), a developer would hit exactly that reaction when comparing analyze.ts to its siblings. Scoring at 79 reflects real convention drift at the behavioral level despite clean surface conventions.

error_consistency.dimension_character: Error handling in this codebase has a correct layer-based distinction (scripts console.error + exit, templates throw) but is undermined by pervasive gaps in HTTP response validation and inconsistent failure modes. Six of seven scripts skip res.ok checks before parsing JSON, creating brittle failure paths. Template code mixes throwing with silent error swallowing (getRecentLaunches returns []), and the trading bot's poll loop silently swallows errors without back-off. A developer writing error handling cannot predict whether a failure will throw, return null, return empty data, or exit the process.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The layer distinction (scripts exit, templates throw) is sound and would push toward 80. However, the missing res.ok checks across 6 scripts and 3 template raw-fetch calls is a systematic gap that means HTTP failures produce confusing JSON parse errors instead of meaningful messages. The silent error swallowing in the trading bot and getRecentLaunches creating ambiguous empty arrays further erode predictability. Per the 60 anchor ('failures are hard to trace'), this sits slightly above because the layer pattern exists, but the gaps are widespread enough to land around 65.

naming_quality.dimension_character: Naming quality in this codebase is generally strong. File names map to capabilities, exported function names are specific and behavior-revealing (createFeeShareConfig, prepareLaunch, getClaimablePositions), and constants use standard domain vocabulary (WSOL, API_BASE). The few weak spots are local variables (entries, pos, flags) that lean generic but are unambiguous within their small scope. No name/behavior mismatches were found; no functions named getX that mutate state.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The exported API names are precise and domain-specific, which is where naming matters most. A developer reading function signatures can predict behavior without checking implementations. The generic local variables (entries, flags) are minor friction points confined to small scopes. Per the 90 anchor ('names are mostly precise; a few generic names require a second look'), this falls slightly below because the generic locals appear in multiple scripts, creating a mild pattern of imprecision. Scoring at 84 reflects strong public naming with minor local name genericness.

abstraction_fitness.dimension_character: The codebase is lean and mostly avoids unnecessary abstraction. Scripts interact directly with APIs, and template bags-client files generally add real value through validation or multi-step orchestration. The one exception is three thin SDK wrappers in the fee-sharing-app client that forward calls without adding behavior, which is a minor cost in a small codebase.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Abstractions are generally earned and purposeful, placing this codebase in the strong range. The prepareLaunch orchestration function is a good example of abstraction that pays for itself. The thin wrappers in fee-sharing-app bags-client prevent a higher score, but they represent a localized issue in an otherwise direct codebase. Score of 88 reflects strong abstraction fitness with minor friction.

dependency_health.dimension_character: The dependency set is lean and purposeful across the codebase. Each template includes only what it needs for its stated purpose, with no duplicate libraries or heavy dependencies pulled in for light use. The only friction is Privy being declared but unused in two templates, which is a minor speculative dependency issue common in starter templates.
dependency_health.score_rationale: Dependencies are well-chosen and cohesive, with no conflicts, duplicates, or heavy deps for light use. The unused Privy dependency in two templates is a real but minor issue. This places the codebase firmly in the strong range at 90, reflecting a dependency set a developer can trust with only isolated friction.

low_level_elegance.dimension_character: Function internals are generally direct and intention-revealing, with consistent patterns across the scripts directory (loadConfig, main().catch wrapper, argument parsing). The template bags-client files show good sequential flow in their orchestration functions. However, there are pockets of defensive complexity (fees.ts response normalization) and silent correctness issues (quote.ts hardcoded decimals) that reduce precision. The inconsistent error handling within token-info.ts shows uneven craft within a single file.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase shows consistent extraction patterns and mostly clear control flow, but the hardcoded decimal assumption in quote.ts is a real correctness concern that would trip up a developer using non-standard tokens. The uneven error handling within token-info.ts and the dense response normalization in fees.ts represent recurring friction. Score of 78 reflects solid foundations with enough local rough edges to place it just below the 80 threshold.

mid_level_elegance.dimension_character: Module boundaries in the templates are clean and predictable, with data translation happening in obvious places. The two-file structure (client + index) is simple and works well. However, the scripts directory repeats identical boundary logic (config loading, API base URL, error patterns) across seven files without a shared module, creating implicit contract duplication. Within the fee-sharing-app, the socialLookup function breaking the SDK boundary pattern introduces a seam inconsistency.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: Template seams are well-designed with explicit, minimal handoffs. The trading-bot orchestration reads cleanly as composition. These strengths push the score up. The duplicated boundary contracts across scripts and the mixed SDK/raw-fetch pattern in fee-sharing-app pull it down. Score of 82 reflects solid seam design with enough inconsistency to be noticeable but not deeply problematic.

test_strategy.dimension_character: There is no test strategy. The codebase has zero test files, zero test configuration, and zero test framework dependencies. Every module, including those handling financial transactions and fee calculations with BigInt arithmetic, is completely untested. A developer making changes to any file has no safety net whatsoever.
test_strategy.score_rationale: With zero test coverage across the entire codebase, including critical financial logic, this dimension is severely deficient. The score is above the absolute floor because this is a dev-kit/template repository where some of the code is instructional scaffolding, and the scripts are CLI tools with limited internal complexity. However, the template bags-client files contain real validation and orchestration logic that developers will copy and extend, making the absence of tests a meaningful quality signal. Score of 15 reflects near-total absence of testing.

api_surface_coherence.dimension_character: The API surface is split between two fundamentally different integration patterns: scripts use raw fetch with manual JSON parsing, while templates use the SDK client. Within templates, the fee-sharing-app uses positional parameters for createFeeShareConfig while the token-launcher uses a config object (LaunchConfig) for a similar domain operation. The trading-bot overloads swap() with a boolean toggle to control execution, mixing concerns in one function.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: A developer would encounter recurring irregularities when moving between modules: different parameter styles (positional vs. config object), mixed SDK/raw-fetch in the same module, and an overloaded swap function. These inconsistencies push the score below 80 (solid but uneven). The scripts having a different pattern from templates is somewhat acceptable as they serve different purposes, but the within-template inconsistencies are harder to justify.

authorization_consistency.dimension_character: Authorization patterns are applied consistently and uniformly across the codebase. All scripts use the same loadConfig() pattern, all templates validate environment variables at module scope before use, and the setup script applies correct filesystem permissions. The codebase has a simple, predictable auth model (API key in header) that a developer can trust.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: A developer can trust that auth is uniformly applied. The consistent loadConfig pattern in scripts and env-var validation in templates demonstrate intentional, reliable auth handling. Minor friction exists in the non-null assertion usage in raw-fetch fallbacks, but this is cosmetic given the module-scope guard. Score reflects mostly consistent with limited, documented exceptions.

ai_generated_debt.dimension_character: The codebase shows several hallmarks of AI-generated code: extensive boilerplate duplication (loadConfig copied 6 times, identical error handlers across all scripts), pass-through wrapper functions in the fee-sharing-app template that add no logic, and index.ts files that are 88-91% comments with minimal actual code. While the comments are not purely restating (they describe the template's purpose), the ratio of documentation to code is extreme for what are essentially barrel exports.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: A developer would need to mentally filter through significant boilerplate to understand what differs between scripts. The verbatim duplication of loadConfig and error handlers across 6-7 files is the clearest AI-generation signal, as a human author would likely extract shared utilities. Pass-through wrappers like getQuote and getLifetimeFees add naming ceremony without behavior. These patterns place the score around 68, reflecting that generated-style noise materially obscures the real logic, though the core business logic in each script is reasonably clear once found.

incomplete_migration.dimension_character: The codebase shows no significant migration debt. All files are TypeScript, all use consistent API versions, and there are no deprecated symbols or stale compatibility shims. The only trace of dual-pattern code is the raw-fetch fallbacks in template bags-client files for endpoints not yet in the SDK, which is an intentional workaround rather than a stalled migration.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: A developer would find a clean, single-technology codebase with no confusing old/new pattern coexistence. The raw-fetch fallbacks are minor and documented with comments explaining why. This places the score at 90: strong with only minor legacy residue that does not cause confusion.

package_organization.dimension_character: The directory layout is clean and purpose-driven. A developer can immediately understand what lives where: scripts for CLI tools, templates for starter apps, references for documentation, commands for skill definitions. Each template is properly isolated with its own package.json. The structure serves navigation well for a project of this size.
package_organization.score_rationale: A developer can predict where to find and place things based on directory structure alone. The only minor friction is the absence of a shared utilities module under scripts/, but per the skip rules, this is a small project (<20 files) where flat structure is appropriate. The clear separation of concerns across top-level directories and the consistent template structure push this toward a strong score. Score of 87 reflects mostly coherent with a minor organizational opportunity.

initialization_coupling.dimension_character: The template bags-client modules all perform environment variable reads and singleton construction at import time, meaning any consumer that imports them must have the correct env vars set or the process crashes immediately. This is a systemic pattern across all three templates, making testing and conditional usage fragile. The scripts/ directory avoids this problem by deferring config loading to function call time.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: Three of six source directories have import-time side effects that throw on missing env vars and construct global singletons with order-sensitive state. This is not a minor rough edge but a recurring pattern that would force a developer to mock environment variables before any import in tests. The scripts/ pattern of deferred loading is correct, which prevents the score from being lower. Anchoring near 60 for significant drag on testability and import safety.

design_coherence.dimension_character: The codebase has a clear structural pattern (scripts as CLI tools, templates as SDK wrappers) but the scripts repeat substantial boilerplate that should be data-driven or extracted to shared infrastructure. Within individual files, some functions mix multiple responsibilities (token-info.ts main() is effectively three scripts in one). The template bags-client files show the opposite problem: some functions are pure pass-throughs that add no value beyond the underlying SDK call.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The repeated boilerplate across 7 script files is the dominant design coherence issue. Each script re-implements loadConfig, API_BASE, CONFIG_PATH, and the main().catch() error handler identically. This is not a single rough edge but a systemic structural pattern that would cause a developer to wonder why these are not shared. The multi-responsibility main() in token-info.ts and the pass-through wrappers in fee-sharing-app further pull the score down. Anchoring around 72 for mixed quality.

contract_coherence.dimension_character: Function contracts are generally honest in intent but imprecise in specification. The scripts communicate their purpose clearly through docstrings and usage comments, but return types are often implicit or overly broad. The template SDK wrappers delegate contract responsibility to the underlying SDK without narrowing or documenting what the caller should expect. The quote.ts decimal assumption is explicitly documented as inaccurate, which is honest but still creates a contract violation at runtime.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase is mostly honest about what functions do, but there are recurring gaps where return types are unspecified, error contracts are implicit, and assumptions (like token decimals) are documented as wrong but not fixed. A developer would need to trace through SDK calls to understand return shapes. This is not severely misleading but creates enough friction to anchor around 74.

logic_clarity.dimension_character: Control flow is generally direct and readable across the codebase. Most functions have a clear linear flow with well-named intermediates. The main issues are three async functions that never await (unnecessary promise wrapping), and some places where nested ternaries or multi-branch conditionals could be simplified. No dead code paths, always-true conditions, or identical branches were found.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase has mostly clear logic with no severe clarity problems. The async-without-await pattern in fee-sharing-app is a genuine logic clarity issue that affects three functions. The nested ternary in fees.ts is less clear than it could be but is not misleading. Overall the logic is traceable and predictable, placing it solidly in the 'mostly clear with isolated simplification opportunities' range around 78.

type_safety.dimension_character: The codebase has a split personality on type safety. Template parameters are generally well-typed (LaunchConfig interface, explicit parameter types on createFeeShareConfig and swap), but return types are almost universally missing. Scripts use explicit 'any' for API response data and Record<string, unknown> for assembled output objects. A developer consuming these functions cannot know what they return without reading the implementation.
type_safety.score_rationale: Missing return types on public functions is a codebase-wide pattern affecting all 13 files. Two scripts use explicit 'any' for API data without defining response types. The LaunchConfig interface and parameter typing are bright spots, but the overall picture is that type annotations do not reliably document runtime behavior. This anchors around 65 for significant type safety gaps that would force a developer to verify behavior independently.

issues.summary: loadConfig(), API_BASE, and CONFIG_PATH duplicated across all 7 scripts
issues.suggestion: Extract loadConfig(), API_BASE, and CONFIG_PATH into a scripts/shared.ts module and import from there. This removes ~15 lines of duplication per script and centralizes the config path.
evidence: Identical loadConfig() function (lines 18-25) appears in all 7 script files with the same try/catch, readFileSync, and error message
API_BASE = 'https://public-api-v2.bags.fm/api/v1' duplicated in 6 of 7 scripts (all except setup.ts)
CONFIG_PATH = join(homedir(), '.bags-dev-kit', 'config.json') duplicated in all 7 scripts

issues.summary: Template bags-client files bypass SDK with raw fetch, duplicating API access patterns
issues.suggestion: Document which SDK methods are missing that force raw fetch fallbacks. If these are SDK gaps, file upstream issues. If the SDK supports these endpoints, switch to SDK methods for consistency.
evidence: fee-sharing-app/src/bags-client.ts socialLookup() uses raw fetch to the same API endpoint as scripts/social-lookup.ts
trading-bot/src/bags-client.ts getRecentLaunches() uses raw fetch with hardcoded URL 'https://public-api-v2.bags.fm/api/v1/token-launch/feed'

issues.summary: socialLookup() in fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts is not specific to fee sharing
issues.suggestion: If socialLookup is needed across multiple templates, note it as a shared utility in documentation. For now, a comment explaining why it lives here (e.g., 'fee sharing requires resolving social handles to wallets for splits') would clarify the placement.
evidence: The socialLookup function (lines 74-86) performs a wallet lookup by social media handle, which is a general-purpose utility not specific to the fee-sharing domain

issues.summary: analyze.ts uses safeFetch() wrapper while all sibling scripts use inline fetch checks
issues.suggestion: Either adopt the safeFetch pattern in all scripts (if the null-return style is preferred) or inline the fetch calls in analyze.ts to match siblings. Pick one protocol and apply it uniformly.
evidence: scripts/analyze.ts defines safeFetch() (lines 27-44) that returns null on failure, while all 5 other API-calling scripts use inline 'const res = await fetch(...); const data = await res.json(); if (!data.success)' pattern

issues.summary: Template index.ts serves different roles: pure re-export (fee-sharing, token-launcher) vs. application logic (trading-bot)
issues.suggestion: This is acceptable variation given the different template types (library-style vs. application-style). Add a brief comment in each index.ts explaining its role pattern (re-export barrel vs. application entry point) to make the intentional variation explicit.
evidence: fee-sharing-app/src/index.ts is 22 lines, 20 of which are comments, with a single export line
token-launcher/src/index.ts is 24 lines, 21 of which are comments, with two export lines
trading-bot/src/index.ts is 52 lines with actual application logic: a polling loop, Set-based dedup, and interval scheduling

issues.summary: 6 of 7 scripts and 3 template raw-fetch calls skip res.ok check before res.json()
issues.suggestion: Add 'if (!res.ok) { console.error(`API error (${res.status})`); process.exit(1); }' before res.json() in scripts, and 'if (!res.ok) throw new Error(`API error: ${res.status} ${res.statusText}`)' in template raw-fetch calls. The safeFetch pattern in analyze.ts already does this correctly.
evidence: fees.ts line 43: 'const data = await res.json()' immediately after fetch, no res.ok check
pools.ts lines 49 and 63: same pattern
quote.ts line 66: same pattern
social-lookup.ts line 58: same pattern
token-info.ts lines 52-53, 64, 73: same pattern across all three code paths
fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts socialLookup() line 83: res.json() without res.ok
token-launcher bags-client.ts checkDexscreenerAvailability() line 97: same
trading-bot bags-client.ts getRecentLaunches() line 89: same
Only analyze.ts safeFetch() (line 30) checks res.ok before parsing

issues.summary: Trading bot poll loop catches and logs errors without recovery or back-off
issues.suggestion: Add a consecutive error counter. After N consecutive failures (e.g., 3), increase the poll interval or pause polling with a clear log message. This prevents silent infinite retry loops.
evidence: Line 37: 'catch (error) { console.error("Poll error:", error); }' silently swallows the error and the next poll fires 10 seconds later regardless
No back-off, no error counter, no circuit breaker. Repeated API failures (rate limit, auth expiry) will spam the console indefinitely

issues.summary: getRecentLaunches() returns empty array on API failure, indistinguishable from no launches
issues.suggestion: Throw on !data.success to let the caller's catch block handle the error, or return a discriminated result type like { launches: Token[], error?: string }.
evidence: Line 90: 'return data.success ? data.response : []' converts API errors to empty arrays
Callers (checkForNewLaunches) treat empty array as 'nothing to do', masking API failures

issues.summary: Error messages across scripts use inconsistent formatting and vocabulary
issues.suggestion: Standardize error message format across scripts, e.g., '[script-name] Error: {data.error || "Unknown error"}'. The varying formats make log parsing and debugging harder than necessary.
evidence: fees.ts: 'Failed to fetch fees:'
pools.ts: 'Pool lookup failed:' and 'Pool list failed:'
quote.ts: 'Quote failed:'
social-lookup.ts: 'Lookup failed:'
token-info.ts: 'Failed:' (bare, no context)
All use 'data.error || "Unknown error"' but with different prefixes and levels of specificity

issues.summary: Variable 'entries' in fees.ts is generic for what represents normalized claimable positions
issues.suggestion: Rename to 'positionEntries' or 'claimableEntries' to communicate that these are normalized fee position records.
evidence: Line 62: 'const entries = Array.isArray(positions) ? positions.map(...) : ...' normalizes the response into key-value pairs, then iterates as 'for (const [key, pos] of entries)'. The name 'entries' reveals nothing about what is being iterated.

issues.summary: Three functions in fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts are pure SDK forwarding wrappers with no added logic
issues.suggestion: Either remove these thin wrappers and have consumers call the SDK client (bags) directly, or add meaningful behavior such as input validation, error enrichment, or response transformation to justify the indirection.
evidence: getQuote() on line 48-54 forwards directly to bags.trade.getQuote with identical parameters
getClaimablePositions() on line 59-61 forwards directly to bags.fee.getAllClaimablePositions
getLifetimeFees() on line 66-68 forwards directly to bags.state.getTokenLifetimeFees

issues.summary: Privy auth dependency declared in fee-sharing-app and token-launcher but never imported in source code
issues.suggestion: Remove @privy-io/react-auth from both package.json files. If Privy integration is intended for users to add themselves, document it in a comment in the template's index.ts or in the .env.example instead of pre-installing the dependency.
evidence: Both package.json files list "@privy-io/react-auth": "^2.5.0" in dependencies
Neither templates/fee-sharing-app/src/bags-client.ts nor templates/fee-sharing-app/src/index.ts import from @privy-io/react-auth
Neither templates/token-launcher/src/bags-client.ts nor templates/token-launcher/src/index.ts import from @privy-io/react-auth

issues.summary: quote.ts assumes all non-SOL tokens have 6 decimals, silently producing incorrect amounts for tokens with other decimal counts
issues.suggestion: At minimum, add a --decimals flag to override the default. Better: fetch the token's actual decimals from the Solana RPC (connection.getParsedAccountInfo) before computing amounts, or clearly document in the output that amounts are approximate for non-6-decimal tokens.
evidence: Line 53: 'const decimals = inputMint === WSOL ? 9 : 6;' assumes all non-SOL SPL tokens use 6 decimals
Line 77: 'const outDecimals = outputMint === WSOL ? 9 : 6;' repeats the same assumption for output
The comment on line 51-52 acknowledges this limitation but the code proceeds with the assumption anyway

issues.summary: token-info.ts --mint branch skips API success/error checking while sibling branches check data.success
issues.suggestion: Add the same data.success check pattern used in the --top and default branches to the --mint branch. Check both creatorsRes.ok and feesRes.ok, and check creators.success and fees.success before using the response data.
evidence: Lines 47-62: the --mint branch calls fetch and immediately calls .json() on both responses without checking res.ok or data.success
Lines 64-70: the --top branch checks data.success and exits on failure
Lines 72-95: the default (feed) branch also checks data.success and exits on failure
This means a 404 or 500 response in the --mint branch would produce confusing output rather than a clear error message

issues.summary: fees.ts uses a dense ternary chain to normalize array/object API response shapes inline
issues.suggestion: Extract to a named helper function like normalizePositionEntries(positions) that makes the three cases explicit with early returns. This makes the shape-handling logic self-documenting and easier to modify if the API changes.
evidence: Lines 62-66: 'const entries = Array.isArray(positions) ? positions.map((pos: any, i: number) => [String(i), pos]) : positions && typeof positions === "object" ? Object.entries(positions) : [];'
This multi-line ternary handles three possible shapes (array, object, falsy) in a single expression that is hard to parse visually

issues.summary: All 7 scripts duplicate identical loadConfig, API_BASE, and CONFIG_PATH definitions instead of sharing a common module
issues.suggestion: Extract a scripts/shared.ts module with API_BASE, CONFIG_PATH, and loadConfig(). Each script imports from it. This reduces the boundary contract to one authoritative definition without adding unnecessary abstraction.
evidence: API_BASE = 'https://public-api-v2.bags.fm/api/v1' is defined identically in 6 scripts (all except setup.ts)
CONFIG_PATH = join(homedir(), '.bags-dev-kit', 'config.json') appears in all 7 scripts
loadConfig() with identical try/catch/JSON.parse/process.exit(1) logic appears in 6 scripts
If the API base URL or config path changes, all files must be updated independently

issues.summary: socialLookup in fee-sharing-app uses raw fetch while sibling functions use the SDK, creating an inconsistent API boundary
issues.suggestion: This is acceptable as a temporary workaround for an SDK gap. Add a TODO comment with an SDK issue reference so it can be updated when the SDK adds this endpoint. Alternatively, move the raw fetch call to a separate utility to make the boundary difference explicit.
evidence: getQuote, getClaimablePositions, getLifetimeFees all use bags.trade/bags.fee/bags.state SDK methods
socialLookup (line 74-86) uses raw fetch() with a hardcoded URL and manual API key header
The comment 'Uses raw fetch since this endpoint is not in the SDK' explains the reason but the result is a mixed boundary pattern in the same module

issues.summary: No test files exist for any module in the repository, including financial transaction and fee calculation logic
issues.suggestion: Start with unit tests for the validation logic that developers will copy: (1) createFeeShareConfig split validation, (2) prepareLaunch config validation, (3) fee arithmetic in analyze.ts. Use vitest for its TypeScript-native support. Mock the SDK client for bags-client tests. Add vitest as a devDependency to each template package.json.
evidence: No .test.ts, .spec.ts, or __tests__ directories exist anywhere in the repository
No test framework (jest, vitest, mocha) configured in any package.json devDependencies
templates/token-launcher/src/bags-client.ts prepareLaunch() has validation logic (splits length matching, basis points totaling 10000) that is never tested
templates/fee-sharing-app/src/bags-client.ts createFeeShareConfig() has the same validation logic untested
scripts/analyze.ts and scripts/fees.ts perform BigInt fee arithmetic with no test coverage
scripts/quote.ts decimal conversion logic (with known hardcoded assumption) is untested

issues.summary: fee-sharing-app bags-client mixes SDK calls with raw fetch for socialLookup
issues.suggestion: Either wrap socialLookup in a comment explaining this is intentional because the endpoint is not in the SDK, or create a raw-fetch helper that mirrors the SDK error contract (throw on failure) so the pattern is explicit rather than ad-hoc.
evidence: Lines 74-86: socialLookup uses raw fetch with hardcoded URL while all other functions in the same module use the bags SDK client

issues.summary: createFeeShareConfig uses 4 positional params while prepareLaunch uses a config object for similar operations
issues.suggestion: Align on the config-object pattern: define a FeeShareConfig interface with payer, baseMint, claimers, and splits fields, matching the LaunchConfig pattern used in the token-launcher template.
evidence: fee-sharing-app createFeeShareConfig(payer, baseMint, claimers, splits) vs token-launcher prepareLaunch(config: LaunchConfig) for operations in the same domain

issues.summary: swap() function overloaded via boolean flag to control quote-only vs execute behavior
issues.suggestion: Split into getSwapQuote(inputMint, outputMint, amountLamports) and executeSwap(inputMint, outputMint, amountLamports) so callers get a clear contract for each operation.
evidence: Line 38: execute = false parameter toggles between returning a quote and actually executing the swap, mixing two distinct responsibilities in one function

issues.summary: Raw fetch paths use process.env.BAGS_API_KEY! bypassing the module-scope check
issues.suggestion: Extract the validated API key into a module-scope constant (e.g., const API_KEY = process.env.BAGS_API_KEY) after the guard check, and reference that constant in raw fetch calls instead of re-reading process.env with a non-null assertion.
evidence: fee-sharing-app/bags-client.ts line 81: process.env.BAGS_API_KEY! in socialLookup raw fetch
trading-bot/bags-client.ts line 87: process.env.BAGS_API_KEY! in getRecentLaunches raw fetch

issues.summary: Identical loadConfig() function copied verbatim across 6 script files
issues.suggestion: Extract loadConfig, API_BASE, and CONFIG_PATH into a shared scripts/shared.ts module that all scripts import from. This eliminates 6 copies of identical code.
evidence: Each file contains the same 7-line loadConfig function: try { return JSON.parse(readFileSync(CONFIG_PATH, 'utf-8')); } catch { console.error(...); process.exit(1); }

issues.summary: Identical main().catch() error handler copied across all 6 async scripts
issues.suggestion: If extracting a shared module, include a runScript(fn) wrapper that handles the catch pattern, or simply accept it as minimal CLI boilerplate. The duplication is low-cost individually but the pattern across all files signals formulaic generation.
evidence: Every script ends with: main().catch((err) => { console.error('Error:', err instanceof Error ? err.message : err); process.exit(1); });

issues.summary: Three functions in fee-sharing-app bags-client are pure pass-through wrappers adding no logic
issues.suggestion: Remove these wrappers and have consumers call bags.trade.getQuote, bags.fee.getAllClaimablePositions, and bags.state.getTokenLifetimeFees directly. The bags client is already exported. Keep createFeeShareConfig which adds validation logic.
evidence: getQuote forwards 3 args to bags.trade.getQuote, getClaimablePositions forwards 1 arg to bags.fee.getAllClaimablePositions, getLifetimeFees forwards 1 arg to bags.state.getTokenLifetimeFees - no added validation, transformation, or error handling

issues.summary: Template index.ts files are 88-91% comments with only 1-2 lines of actual code
issues.suggestion: Move the template documentation to a README.md in each template directory. Keep the index.ts files as clean barrel exports with at most a 1-line comment describing the template.
evidence: fee-sharing-app/src/index.ts: 22 lines, of which 20 are comments/whitespace and 1 is a re-export. token-launcher/src/index.ts: 24 lines, 21 comments/whitespace, 2 re-exports

issues.summary: Two template modules fall back to raw fetch for endpoints not in the SDK
issues.suggestion: Track these as SDK feature gaps. When @bagsfm/bags-sdk adds support for these endpoints, replace the raw fetch calls with SDK methods. Consider adding a TODO with an SDK version target.
evidence: fee-sharing-app/bags-client.ts line 73 comment: 'Uses raw fetch since this endpoint is not in the SDK'
trading-bot/bags-client.ts line 86: getRecentLaunches uses raw fetch to hit /token-launch/feed

issues.summary: scripts/ directory lacks a shared module for common utilities duplicated across all scripts
issues.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: (1) Create scripts/shared.ts exporting loadConfig(), API_BASE, and CONFIG_PATH. (2) Update all 6 scripts to import from './shared'. (3) Verify with: npx tsx scripts/analyze.ts --help (or similar) for each script to confirm imports resolve. This is a single-session change with no cross-directory impact.
evidence: All 6 async scripts duplicate loadConfig(), API_BASE constant, and CONFIG_PATH constant. A scripts/shared.ts would centralize these and reduce the directory from 7 to 8 files while eliminating ~36 lines of duplication.

issues.summary: All template bags-client.ts files throw on missing env vars at import time
issues.suggestion: Export a factory function (e.g., createBagsClient()) or use lazy initialization via a getter that creates the client on first access. This allows importing the module without requiring env vars to be set, which is critical for testing and conditional usage.
evidence: Line 8-9 in all three files: if (!process.env.BAGS_API_KEY) throw new Error(...) executes at module scope
templates/trading-bot/src/bags-client.ts lines 13-15: additionally throws on missing SOLANA_PRIVATE_KEY at import time

issues.summary: Trading bot creates Connection and Keypair singletons at module scope
issues.suggestion: Defer Connection and Keypair creation to a lazy getter or factory function. The wallet singleton is especially fragile since it decodes a private key at import time, which crashes if the env var is malformed.
evidence: Line 21-23: export const connection = new Connection(...) at module scope
Line 25-27: export const wallet = Keypair.fromSecretKey(bs58.decode(process.env.SOLANA_PRIVATE_KEY)) at module scope

issues.summary: All 7 scripts duplicate loadConfig, constants, and error handling boilerplate
issues.suggestion: Extract a shared scripts/common.ts with loadConfig(), API_BASE, CONFIG_PATH, and a runScript(fn) wrapper that handles the main().catch() boilerplate. Each script becomes a focused function.
evidence: loadConfig() is copy-pasted identically in 6 of 7 scripts (setup.ts has its own variant)
API_BASE and CONFIG_PATH constants are identical in all 7 files
main().catch((err) => { console.error(...); process.exit(1); }) is identical in 6 scripts

issues.summary: token-info.ts main() implements three distinct commands in one function body
issues.suggestion: Split into three focused functions (fetchTokenCreators, fetchTopTokens, fetchTokenFeed) called from main() based on the flag. This makes each path independently readable and testable.
evidence: Lines 45-96: three independent branches (--mint, --top, default feed) each with their own fetch, parse, and format logic, sharing only the config and headers setup

issues.summary: Three async functions in fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts never use await
issues.suggestion: Remove the async keyword from these three functions since they simply return the SDK promise. The async wrapper adds no value and creates a redundant promise wrapping.
evidence: getQuote (line 48-53), getClaimablePositions (line 59-61), getLifetimeFees (line 66-68) are async but just return the SDK call directly without await

issues.summary: scripts/quote.ts duplicates loadConfig and boilerplate found across all scripts
issues.suggestion: Covered by repeated_script_boilerplate issue. Extract to shared module.
evidence: Lines 19-29: identical loadConfig + CONFIG_PATH + API_BASE as other scripts

issues.reasoning: The console.error + process.exit pattern is the standard CLI error reporting approach in this codebase. The top-level main().catch() handles uncaught errors. This is acceptable for scripts.
console.error + process.exit(1) is the deliberate error reporting pattern for CLI scripts in this codebase
A polling loop that catches and logs errors is the correct pattern for a long-running bot that should survive transient API failures

issues.summary: analyze.ts safeFetch has no return type annotation despite returning data | null
issues.suggestion: Add explicit return type: Promise<unknown | null> or a generic Promise<T | null> with runtime validation. At minimum, the null return contract should be visible in the signature.
evidence: Line 27: async function safeFetch(url: string, headers: Record<string, string>) has no return type
Function returns data.response (unknown shape) or null on any error path

issues.summary: quote.ts documents its decimal assumption as inaccurate but uses it anyway
issues.suggestion: Either fetch the token's actual decimals from the API before computing the amount, or make the function accept a decimals parameter and document that callers must provide it. The current code silently miscomputes for non-standard tokens.
evidence: Lines 51-53: comment says 'For accurate conversion, check the token's actual decimals on-chain' but then hardcodes const decimals = inputMint === WSOL ? 9 : 6
This silently produces wrong amounts for tokens with non-6 decimal places

issues.summary: prepareLaunch return type does not communicate that transactions array shape varies
issues.suggestion: Add an explicit return type with a transactions field typed as an array, and document in the docstring that the array contains fee config transactions followed by the launch transaction.
evidence: Line 82: ...(feeConfig.transactions || []) means the transactions array may contain 0 to N fee config transactions plus one launch transaction, but the return type does not distinguish these

issues.summary: Three async functions in fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts never use await
issues.suggestion: Remove the async keyword from all three functions. They return the SDK's promise directly and gain nothing from the async wrapper. The extra promise layer is confusing because it suggests the function body contains awaited operations.
evidence: getQuote (line 48): async function that returns bags.trade.getQuote() without await
getClaimablePositions (line 59): async function that returns bags.fee.getAllClaimablePositions() without await
getLifetimeFees (line 66): async function that returns bags.state.getTokenLifetimeFees() without await

issues.summary: scripts/fees.ts uses nested ternary for response shape detection
issues.suggestion: Replace with explicit if/else blocks or extract a normalizePositions(positions) helper that returns a uniform array shape. This makes the three cases (array, object, null/undefined) individually readable.
evidence: Lines 62-66: const entries = Array.isArray(positions) ? positions.map(...) : positions && typeof positions === 'object' ? Object.entries(positions) : [] is a two-level nested ternary

issues.summary: No script function has a return type annotation
issues.suggestion: Add explicit return types to at least loadConfig (: { apiKey: string; defaultWallet?: string }), safeFetch (: Promise<unknown>), and parseArgs functions. Define a BagsConfig interface for loadConfig's return type.
evidence: loadConfig() in all scripts: no return type (returns parsed JSON as any)
main() in all scripts: no return type (returns Promise<void> implicitly)
safeFetch() in analyze.ts: no return type (returns unknown | null)
parseArgs() in pools.ts and token-info.ts: no return type (returns Record<string, string | boolean>)

issues.summary: Scripts use explicit 'any' for API response data without defining types
issues.suggestion: Define lightweight interfaces for the API response shapes used in these scripts (e.g., interface ClaimablePosition { tokenMint: string; type: string; claimableLamports: string; } and interface TokenFeedItem { name: string; symbol: string; tokenMint: string; ... }). These replace 'any' with meaningful types.
evidence: fees.ts line 63: (pos: any, i: number) in positions.map callback
token-info.ts line 84: (t: any) in tokens.map callback

issues.summary: Template public functions lack return type annotations
issues.suggestion: Add return types to all exported functions. For swap(), define a discriminated union type (SwapResult = SwapQuoteOnly | SwapSimulationFailed | SwapExecuted) to make the three return shapes explicit. For prepareLaunch(), define a LaunchResult interface.
evidence: prepareLaunch in token-launcher: returns a complex object with tokenMint, metadata, feeShareAuthority, configKey, transactions but no return type
swap in trading-bot: returns three different shapes ({ quote, executed: false }, { quote, executed: false, error }, { quote, executed: true, bundleId }) but no return type or discriminated union
getRecentLaunches in trading-bot: returns data.response or empty array with no type annotation

issues.summary: analyze.ts uses Record<string, unknown> for the analysis result object
issues.suggestion: Define an AnalysisResult interface with explicit fields (tokenMint, lifetimeFees, creators, claimStats, pool, feeAnalysis?) to replace Record<string, unknown>.
evidence: Line 64: const analysis: Record<string, unknown> = { ... } throws away all type information for the assembled output
Line 92: analysis.feeAnalysis = { ... } adds a property dynamically, which Record<string, unknown> allows but obscures the structure

add.header: Clean template isolation with no cross-imports
add.description: Each of the three templates (fee-sharing-app, token-launcher, trading-bot) is fully self-contained with its own bags-client.ts and index.ts. No template imports from another template, giving clean change boundaries.

add.header: Scripts are independent standalone CLI tools
add.description: All 7 scripts in scripts/ have zero fan-in and zero fan-out to each other, operating as independent CLI entry points. This is architecturally clean but results in duplicated infrastructure.

add.header: No circular dependencies or hub modules
add.description: With only index.ts importing bags-client.ts in each template, the import graph is trivially acyclic. No module acts as a hub with high blast radius.
scripts/ maps to CLI exploration tools and templates/ maps to starter app scaffolds. Each directory name communicates its purpose. A new engineer can navigate by directory name alone.

All three templates follow the same bags-client.ts + index.ts pattern. bags-client.ts owns the API surface, index.ts re-exports and documents. This consistency makes the pattern learnable from one template.
Each script file maps to exactly one Bags API capability (fees, pools, quotes, etc.). File names are the API verbs. This makes discovery trivial.

add.header: Consistent camelCase naming across all files
add.description: Both scripts/ and templates/ use camelCase for functions and variables uniformly. No snake_case drift detected in any file.

add.header: Consistent named export pattern across templates
add.description: All template bags-client.ts files use named exports exclusively. No default exports, no mixed patterns. index.ts files use named re-exports.
All 7 scripts follow the same structural skeleton: shebang, JSDoc, imports, constants, loadConfig, main, error handler. This makes the files predictable at the structure level.

add.header: Behavioral protocol drift in API error handling
add.description: analyze.ts uses a safeFetch wrapper returning null while siblings use inline fetch-then-check. This is a protocol-level inconsistency despite consistent surface structure.

Scripts use console.error + process.exit(1) for user-facing CLI errors, while template bags-client.ts files throw errors for programmatic callers. This is a deliberate and appropriate layer-based distinction.

add.header: Systematic missing res.ok checks before JSON parsing
add.description: Nearly all fetch calls (8 of 9 raw fetch call sites) skip checking res.ok before calling res.json(). Only analyze.ts safeFetch does this correctly. HTTP 500 or network errors will produce confusing JSON parse failures.

Template bags-client.ts files validate inputs before making API calls (e.g., checking claimers.length === splits.length, splits summing to 10000). This is a positive pattern for catching errors early.

Public function names consistently use domain vocabulary: createFeeShareConfig, prepareLaunch, getClaimablePositions, getLifetimeFees, checkDexscreenerAvailability. A developer can predict behavior from the name alone.

add.header: File names map to API capabilities
add.description: Script file names (analyze, fees, pools, quote, setup, social-lookup, token-info) directly communicate which Bags API capability the script exercises. No generic names like 'utils' or 'helpers'.

Functions use verb prefixes consistently: 'load' for config reading (loadConfig), 'get' for data retrieval (getQuote, getClaimablePositions), 'create' for write operations (createFeeShareConfig). The vocabulary is small and predictable.

The token-launcher prepareLaunch function orchestrates three sequential SDK calls with validation, composing a multi-step workflow into a single coherent operation. This is abstraction that genuinely reduces consumer complexity.

All seven scripts in scripts/ interact directly with fetch and format output inline. There are no abstraction layers, wrapper functions, or intermediate types between the API calls and the output. This directness is appropriate for CLI scripts.

The single custom interface in the codebase (LaunchConfig) serves a clear purpose: it defines the input shape for a complex multi-step operation. It has one consumer and no polymorphic pressure, but it earns its existence by documenting a non-trivial parameter set.

add.header: Lean purposeful dependency sets per template
add.description: Each template includes only the dependencies it actively uses: SDK, web3.js, and framework deps. No duplicate libraries or heavy deps for light use. This makes the templates fast to install and easy to audit.

add.header: Consistent SDK version pinning across templates
add.description: All three templates pin @bagsfm/bags-sdk to the same ^1.3.1 range, preventing version drift between templates that developers might use together as reference.

The scripts/ directory contains standalone .ts files run via npx tsx. They have no dedicated package.json, relying on global tsx availability. This is a deliberate design choice for CLI scripts that avoids dependency management overhead.

All seven scripts follow the same structural pattern: loadConfig() for config loading, main() async function for logic, and main().catch() for top-level error handling. This consistency makes any script immediately familiar after reading one.

add.header: swap() function has clean linear flow
add.description: The trading-bot swap function follows a clear sequential pipeline: quote, conditionally create transaction, sign, simulate, submit. Each step builds on the previous with an early return if execute is false. This is well-structured orchestration.

add.header: safeFetch is the best error handling pattern
add.description: scripts/analyze.ts safeFetch helper shows clean three-layer error checking (HTTP status, API success flag, catch) with URL truncation in messages. This is the gold standard for the scripts directory but is only used in analyze.ts.

Each template uses a simple bags-client.ts + index.ts structure where the client handles API interaction and index.ts defines the public surface. This predictable seam makes it immediately clear where API logic lives versus app entry points.

The token-launcher prepareLaunch function is the single place where a flat LaunchConfig is translated into sequential SDK calls. Consumers never need to know about the multi-step API choreography. This is clean boundary design.

Each script in scripts/ is a standalone executable with no cross-script imports. This is a deliberate design choice for CLI tools but results in duplicated boundary contracts (config loading, API base URL) across seven files.
No test files, test configurations, or test framework dependencies exist anywhere in the repository. This is a structural gap, not an oversight in specific modules. The codebase has never had tests.

add.header: Template validation logic is highest-priority test target
add.description: The bags-client files in templates contain validation logic (split length matching, basis point totals) that developers will copy into their own projects. Testing this logic would serve both as quality assurance and as a testing example for consumers of the dev-kit.

add.header: Scripts use consistent raw fetch pattern
add.description: All 7 scripts follow the same structural pattern: loadConfig, build URL, fetch with x-api-key header, check data.success. This consistency within the scripts layer is a positive signal.
Templates correctly use @bagsfm/bags-sdk as the primary integration layer rather than raw fetch, which provides typed methods and better developer experience.
Scripts use raw fetch (appropriate for standalone CLI tools) while templates use the SDK (appropriate for app code). This is a deliberate layer-appropriate split, not accidental drift.
All 7 scripts use the identical loadConfig() function to read API credentials from ~/.bags-dev-kit/config.json, providing a single predictable auth pattern for CLI tools.

add.header: Module-scope env var validation in templates
add.description: All 3 template bags-client.ts files validate required env vars at module scope with throw, failing fast before any API call can be made without credentials.
setup.ts sets 0o700 on the config directory and 0o600 on the config file, preventing other users from reading the API key.

Script names (analyze, fees, pools, quote, social-lookup, token-info) and function names (safeFetch, resolveMint, prepareLaunch) use domain vocabulary rather than generic names, which is a positive signal against AI-generated naming.
Unlike the pass-through wrappers, createFeeShareConfig validates array length matching and basis point totals before calling the SDK, demonstrating purposeful wrapping.
loadConfig, API_BASE, CONFIG_PATH, and the main().catch() handler are duplicated verbatim across all 6 async scripts, a hallmark of AI-generated code that was never consolidated.
All 13 source files are TypeScript with no JS/TS mixing, indicating no incomplete TypeScript migration.
Code uses v2/v3 endpoints (creator/v3, wallet/v2, claim-txs/v3) matching the latest API reference, with no legacy endpoint versions in use.

add.header: SDK coverage gaps require raw fetch
add.description: Two template modules fall back to raw fetch for /token-launch/feed and /token-launch/fee-share/wallet/v2 because these endpoints are not yet in @bagsfm/bags-sdk. This is a known workaround, not a stalled migration.
scripts/, templates/, references/, commands/ each serve a distinct, non-overlapping concern. A developer can navigate by directory name alone.
Each template subdirectory has its own package.json with template-specific dependencies, preventing cross-template coupling and allowing independent installation.

add.header: Small codebase appropriate for flat structure
add.description: At 13 files and 983 LOC, the codebase is well within the size range where deeper nesting would add navigation overhead without benefit.
All scripts/ files use a loadConfig() function called at runtime rather than import time, meaning imports have no side effects. This is the correct pattern for CLI tools.

add.header: Template modules throw at import time
add.description: All three template bags-client.ts files check environment variables and construct singletons at module scope, making them impossible to import without env vars set. This couples import order to environment state.
The codebase has a clean two-tier structure: scripts/ for standalone CLI tools and templates/ for SDK-wrapping application starters. Each tier has a consistent purpose and internal pattern.
All 7 script files independently re-implement loadConfig, API constants, and the main().catch() error handler. This duplication is the primary design coherence issue in the codebase.

add.header: Console.error + process.exit is standard CLI pattern
add.description: Scripts consistently use console.error followed by process.exit(1) for error reporting. This is a deliberate convention for CLI tools, not a design defect.
All public functions in both scripts and templates have JSDoc comments that accurately describe what the function does. The intent contract is honest even where the type contract is imprecise.

add.header: Return types delegated to SDK without narrowing
add.description: Template bags-client functions return whatever the SDK returns without adding type annotations or narrowing. Callers must trust the SDK's types or inspect them manually.

add.header: Validation at function boundaries is correct
add.description: createFeeShareConfig and prepareLaunch both validate their inputs (claimers.length === splits.length, splits sum to 10000) before calling the SDK. These guards are honest and match the documented requirements.

add.header: Clear linear flow in script main functions
add.description: Most scripts follow a straightforward pattern: parse args, load config, fetch data, format output. Intermediate variables are well-named and the flow is easy to trace.

add.header: BigInt arithmetic is correct and careful
add.description: Fee calculations in analyze.ts and fees.ts use BigInt for lamport precision and convert to SOL only for display. This avoids floating-point precision issues.
Three functions in fee-sharing-app bags-client.ts are marked async but never await anything, creating unnecessary promise wrapping that misleads readers about the function's complexity.
The token-launcher template defines a clean LaunchConfig interface with appropriate required and optional fields. This is the strongest type safety in the codebase and serves as a good model.
Function parameters across both scripts and templates are properly typed (string, number[], etc.). The type safety gap is primarily in return types, not input types.

No function in the codebase has an explicit return type annotation. Return types are inferred by TypeScript but not documented in the source, making it impossible to understand function contracts from signatures alone.
